chunk_summary: Early initiation of breastfeeding within the first hour stabilizes the newborn, supports lactation, and provides protective colostrum, which is rich in immunological and growth factors.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of initiating breastfeeding within the first hour of birth.
Recognize the biological and immunological benefits of colostrum for newborns.
Identify barriers to early breastfeeding initiation and strategies to overcome them.
Learn alternative feeding methods, such as hand expression and cup feeding, when direct latching is not possible.
key_facts: Breastfeeding within the first hour stabilizes the newborn and promotes thermal regulation and cardiorespiratory stability.
Colostrum contains secretory IgA, lactoferrin, leukocytes, and growth factors that protect the gut and reduce infections.
Colostrum volume matches the newborn's gastric capacity and should not be judged as insufficient.
Delays in first feeding are often caused by separation, maternal fatigue, or misconceptions about milk production.
Prelacteal feeds reduce suckling frequency and delay effective milk production.
Early hand expression and spoon/cup feeding of colostrum can protect lactation and reduce formula exposure.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Early initiation of breastfeeding within the first hour stabilizes the newborn and establishes effective lactation. (Source: WHO BFHI, https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241513807)
